ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general biryani food product class (FOODON_00002700); no Mysore-specific or gosht-specific subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKM covers biryani as a rice dish with meat; no Mysore gosht-specific code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for biryani or Mysore gosht biryani as a food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Mysore Gosht Biryani is approximately 450 g, comprising a generous portion of spiced basmati rice layered with mutton, served with raita and gravy on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Biryani — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports nutrient values for mutton biryani (cooked, per 100 g edible portion). Mysore Gosht Biryani is a regional variant of mutton biryani using Mysore-style spices and dum cooking; the macronutrient profile is closely analogous. Values adapted from ICMR-NIN mutton biryani entry. Serving with raita and gravy adds minor sodium and dairy contributions captured in sodium and fat estimates.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mysore Gosht Biryani. GI of 58 is estimated from the closest analogous food: basmati rice-based biryani with meat, referenced in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Basmati rice has a GI of approximately 57–58 (medium); the addition of fat and protein from mutton and oil lowers the effective GI slightly relative to plain rice. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 92.25) / 100 ≈ 53.5, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 20.5 × 4.5 = 92.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~1710 mg per restaurant serving) from spice blends, salt, and accompanying gravy
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a standard serving and request reduced-salt preparation, as a full restaurant serving may contribute approximately 74% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 58) and high glycemic load per restaurant serving (~53) due to large basmati rice portion
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the rice portion to approximately 150–200 g of the total serving and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables or raita to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.4 g per 100 g) from mutton and cooking oil, combined with high sodium from spice blends and gravy
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional servings, choose lean mutton cuts, and request preparation with minimal added oil to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mysore Gosht Biryani is a fragrant South Indian–style mutton biryani originating from Mysore (Mysuru), Karnataka, India. It is prepared by marinating goat or mutton pieces in a Mysore-specific spice blend — typically incorporating dried red chillies, coriander, cumin, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and a distinctive Mysore masala paste — then frying the meat in oil before layering it with partially cooked aromatic basmati rice and slow-cooking the assembled dish using the dum (sealed steam) method. The result is a richly spiced, moist, and aromatic rice dish in which the rice grains remain distinct and are infused with the deep red-orange colour and bold heat characteristic of Mysore cuisine. The mutton is tender and well-seasoned, with a slightly spicier and more robust flavour profile compared to Hyderabadi or Lucknowi biryani styles. In Singapore restaurant contexts, it is typically served as a complete meal with raita (a cooling yoghurt-based condiment with cucumber or onion) and a thin mutton gravy on the side. Nutritionally, a standard restaurant serving (~450 g) provides approximately 774 kcal, 38 g protein, 31 g fat, and 92 g carbohydrate. The dish is a significant source of dietary sodium (~1710 mg per serving) due to salt, spice blends, and gravy, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index (~58) and high glycemic load per full serving require attention for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is halal-suitable when prepared with halal-certified mutton and contains dairy from the raita component.
common_user_queries: How is Mysore mutton biryani different from Hyderabadi biryani?
How many calories in Mysore Gosht Biryani?
What spices are used in Mysore biryani?
Can I eat biryani if I have high blood pressure?
What is dum cooking in biryani?

ingredient_synonyms.dum: sealed steam cooking, slow-cooked under pressure
ingredient_synonyms.raita: yoghurt condiment, cucumber raita, boondi raita
ingredient_synonyms.Mysore masala: Mysore-style spice paste, Karnataka spice blend
ingredient_synonyms.mutton: goat meat, bone-in goat, lamb (in some regional usages)

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mutton biryani data, which is the highest-priority approved source for Indian dishes. Mysore Gosht Biryani is a regional variant with closely analogous macronutrient composition. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (basmati rice-based biryani with meat) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. No exact dish-level measurement exists in any approved primary source for this specific Mysore variant.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN mutton biryani source entry. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN mutton biryani source entry. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN mutton biryani source entry. All other -1 values reflect nutrients not reported in the adapted ICMR-NIN source. Sodium estimate includes contribution from accompanying raita and gravy as served in restaurant context. Halal status reflects typical restaurant preparation; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. The field dataed (Curry Village-Authentic North Indian Cuisine, Boat Quay, Singapore) serves this dish at SGD 22.90 per portion as of the field survey data source record.
